claim: The Eiffel Tower was built in 1889 for the World's Fair in Paris.
NASA confirmed the Moon landing was staged in a Hollywood studio.
Drinking bleach can cure COVID-19 according to WHO.
notes: Dangerous misinformation, WHO never said this

claim: Vaccines cause autism according to a 1998 Lancet study.
notes: The 1998 Wakefield study was retracted and Wakefield lost his medical license

claim: The Great Wall of China is visible from space with the naked eye.
notes: Astronauts have confirmed this is not true

claim: Albert Einstein failed mathematics in school.
notes: Einstein excelled at mathematics — this is a widespread myth

claim: The human body has 206 bones in adulthood.
5G towers spread the coronavirus by weakening immune systems.
notes: No scientific basis; 5G is radio waves and cannot transmit viruses

claim: Climate change is primarily caused by human activities according to scientific consensus.
notes: 97%+ scientific consensus per IPCC and major academies

claim: Eating carrots improves your night vision beyond normal levels.
notes: Carrots help prevent deficiency-related vision loss but don't enhance normal vision; WWII propaganda origin

claim: The Amazon rainforest produces 20% of the world's oxygen.
notes: Often cited but contested — the forest consumes roughly as much oxygen as it produces

claim: Napoleon Bonaparte was unusually short for his time.
notes: Napoleon was 5'6" — average height for a French man of his era; confusion from French/English inch units

claim: Humans only use 10% of their brain.
notes: Brain imaging shows we use virtually all brain regions

claim: Mount Everest is the tallest mountain on Earth measured from sea level.
notes: True when measured from sea level; Mauna Kea is taller from base

claim: Coffee stunts the growth of children and teenagers.
notes: No scientific evidence supports this; likely originates from early caffeine studies
